3A_Bibliographic.citation: Paulich, K. N., Ross, J. M., Lessem, J. M., Hewitt, J. K. (2024). Screen time and early adolescent mental health, academic, and social outcomes in 9- and 10-year old children: Utilizing the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. Pediatric Research.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Participants included in this study were children ages 9-10 years (N = 11,875) from the ABCD Study.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Cross-sectional baseline data only. Sample sizes vary by outcome due to missing data.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Total screen time (hours/day) calculated as weighted average of weekday and weekend screen time across multiple modalities
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV/movies, videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen time was measured using the youth screen time survey... Total screen time was calculated as the average number of hours per day spent on screens across all activities, separately for weekdays and weekends. An overall screen time measure was computed as [(weekday hours x 5) + (weekend hours x 2)]/7.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Multiple outcomes: mental health (internalizing/externalizing), academic performance, social functioning
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) for mental health; ABCD measures for academic and social outcomes
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Mental health outcomes included internalizing and externalizing problems measured via the Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL). Academic outcomes included grades and school engagement. Social outcomes included peer relationships.
3D_Outcome.notes: Multiple outcome domains examined. Mental health (CBCL) is the primary focus.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple linear regression with site as random effect
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, study site
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Linear regression models were used to examine associations between screen time and each outcome, adjusting for demographic covariates including age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, and study site.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Screen time was associated with higher internalizing problems (beta = 0.03, p < 0.05)
Screen time was associated with higher externalizing problems (beta = 0.05, p < 0.05)

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: Screen time was significantly associated with mental health, academic, and social outcomes
3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: This large sample from the ABCD study provides power to detect associations
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: While effect sizes were small, the associations were consistent across outcomes and may have implications at the population level
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Effect sizes are very small (betas around 0.03-0.05). Paper acknowledges small effects but still recommends action.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: These findings support recommendations to monitor and potentially limit screen time in children, particularly given the consistent associations across multiple domains of functioning.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Screen time is a potentially modifiable risk factor for youth mental health and functioning
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses on the direction and consistency of associations across multiple outcome domains. While the paper notes effect sizes are small, it emphasizes the consistency of findings and frames screen time as a modifiable target for intervention. The overall tone suggests clinical significance beyond what the small effect sizes warrant.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Exact effect sizes from the PDF were difficult to extract with full precision. The paper reports many analyses across multiple outcomes. The primary model specification was somewhat ambiguous.
